epigraph.text: If you love me, you will keep my commandments.

content.text:
Mathematical reasoning often takes the form: "If *this* is true, 
then *that* must also be true." Such statements are called 
**conditional statements**, and understanding them is essential for 
constructing and following proofs.

content.title: Conditional Statement
content.content:
A **conditional statement** has the form 
"If $p$, then $q$," written symbolically as $p \rightarrow q$.

- $p$ is the **hypothesis** (the "if" part)

- $q$ is the **conclusion** (the "then" part)

content.title: Identifying hypothesis and conclusion
content.problem:
Identify the hypothesis and conclusion:

"If an angle measures $90°$, then it is a right angle."
content.solution:
Hypothesis ($p$): An angle measures $90°$.

Conclusion ($q$): It is a right angle.

items: **True** when: $p$ is false, OR both $p$ and $q$ are true
**False** only when: $p$ is true and $q$ is false

content.text:
Every conditional has three related statements formed by negating or 
switching hypothesis and conclusion.

content.title: Related Conditionals
content.content:
Given the conditional $p \rightarrow q$ ("If $p$, then $q$"):

- **Converse**: $q \rightarrow p$ 
 ("If $q$, then $p$")

- **Inverse**: $\neg p \rightarrow \neg q$ 
 ("If not $p$, then not $q$")

- **Contrapositive**: $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ 
 ("If not $q$, then not $p$")

content.title: Writing related conditionals
content.problem:
Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive of:

"If a triangle is equilateral, then it has three congruent sides."
content.solution:
**Converse:** If a triangle has three congruent sides, then it is 
equilateral.

**Inverse:** If a triangle is not equilateral, then it does not have 
three congruent sides.

**Contrapositive:** If a triangle does not have three congruent sides, 
then it is not equilateral.

content.title: Contrapositive Equivalence
content.content:
A conditional statement and its contrapositive are logically equivalent: 
they have the same truth value.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This means: if you can prove the contrapositive, you have proven the 
original statement. This technique is called **proof by contrapositive**%
.

**Warning:** The converse and inverse are *not* equivalent to 
the original. A true statement can have a false converse!

content.title: True statement, false converse
content.problem:
Statement: "If an animal is a dog, then it is a mammal." (TRUE)

Converse: "If an animal is a mammal, then it is a dog." (FALSE—cats 
are mammals but not dogs)

content.title: Biconditional
content.content:
A **biconditional** combines a conditional and 
its converse: "$p$ if and only if $q$," written $p \leftrightarrow q$.

A biconditional is true when both the conditional and its converse are true.

content.text: Many definitions in geometry can be written as biconditionals.

content.title: Biconditional
content.problem:
"An angle is a right angle if and only if it measures $90°$."

This combines:

- If an angle is a right angle, then it measures $90°$.

- If an angle measures $90°$, then it is a right angle.

Both are true, so the biconditional is true.

marginNotes.text:
And if you faithfully obey the voice 
of the Lord your God... all these blessings shall come upon you.
